key_insight: Pre-existing natural fractures redirect hydraulic fractures by locally redistributing stress, with the trajectory determined by whether shear deformation increases compressive shielding or promotes NF attraction and linkage.
engineering_relevance: Predicting HF-NF interactions from local stress states can improve subsurface stimulation and storage design by helping engineers anticipate deflection, crossing, or connection with natural fractures.
method: Coupled laboratory experiments on intact and pre-fractured PMMA specimens under plane-strain conditions
Digital image correlation to measure full-field displacement and strain during loading and hydraulic fracturing
Poroelastic extended finite element simulations to reproduce HF curvature and mixed-mode fracture behavior
evidence_signal: The abstract reports consistent experimental and numerical agreement across fixed-base, lateral confinement, and vertical compression cases, with mixed-mode criteria needed to match curved HF paths in pre-fractured specimens.
watch_item: Results are based on PMMA and controlled laboratory boundary conditions, so field-scale geological complexity and heterogeneity may alter the observed HF-NF interaction mechanisms.
paper_title: Local Stress Redistribution Controls Interactions between Hydraulic Fractures and Pre-existing Fractures
